sections.directions: Directions: Each question in this section is based on the reasoning presented in a brief passage. In answering the questions, you should not make assumptions that are by commonsense standards implausible, superfluous, or incompatible with the passage. For some questions, more than one of the choices could conceivably answer the question. However, you are to choose the best answer; that is, choose the response that most accurately and completely answers the question.

items.stimulusText:
Nakagawa: When making business decisions, we who manage the company are obliged to take into account the public interest as well as normal business concerns like efficiency and expense.

Gauldin: I disagree. Our obligation to the company's stockholders requires us to choose the most profitable course of action allowable by law without regard for public interest.
items.stemText: Nakagawa's and Gauldin's remarks provide the most support for holding that they disagree about the truth of which one of the following statements?

options.optionContent: Managers usually try to maximize profit without regard for public interest.
Managers should always do whatever their companies' stockholders want them to do.
Managers should choose to pollute the environment if it is legal and more profitable than not doing so.
Managers should never do what the public wants them to do.
Managers should give equal weight to both profit and public interest in making business decisions.

items.stimulusText: Criminologist: Dr. Ashton claims most people are not responsible for the crimes they commit because of the exploitative nature of the economic system in which they live. He alleges there is empirical evidence of this. His argument may be dismissed since it is exactly what one would expect given Dr. Ashton's political views.
items.stemText: Which one of the following exhibits flawed reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning exhibited by the criminologist's argument?

options.optionContent: Dr. Catalon's case against this proposal should be dismissed. His case proceeded merely by attacking the character of the sponsor of the proposal.

Dr. Ramirez's argument that a new road should be built downtown is unacceptable. If she is allowed to have a new road built today, tomorrow she will want a new parking lot, then a new mall, and soon the town's beautiful surroundings will be covered with cement.

Dr. Narayan argues that there are estimates of traffic volumes that show that the proposed new highway is unnecessary. But we would expect such an argument from Dr. Narayan since he generally believes that no new highways should be built. Thus, his argument merits no consideration.
Dr. Eton's testimony about the footprints found at the crime scene is dubious. She is a close friend of the defendant and so her credibility as a witness at the inquest is suspect.

Dr. Makeba's claim that the universe is already collapsing is based on her own unusual explanation of cosmic background radiation. However, since this explanation is highly controversial, her claim should not be accepted without further investigation.

items.stimulusText: In classic mystery stories, master criminals are captured through the genius of a detective working alone; but in one type of modern detective story—the police procedural—criminals are pursued by uninspired though hardworking people who employ the same mind-numbing routine each day. Hence, the development of the police procedural is an endorsement of the effectiveness of bureaucracies rather than individuals.
items.stemText: Each of the following, if true, provides evidence against the conclusion drawn in the argument except:

options.optionContent: In police procedurals, there is often one member of the force, more clever than the rest, who does the most important work in catching the criminal.
In police procedurals, criminals often go uncaptured because of the ineptness of the law enforcement agencies responsible for pursuing them.
In police procedurals, criminals are often captured when the police are the beneficiaries of critical evidence that turns up during routine investigations.
In police procedurals, crimes are often solved when the normal routine is broken.
In police procedurals, members of the force are often portrayed as defying the rules by which their department is run.

items.stimulusText: Journalist: Those of us acquainted with the work of filmmaker John Dowland are convinced that the accusations made against him of tax fraud and other financial dishonesty are baseless, for we know intimately the honesty and integrity that pervade his art—art that is notably uncommercial.
items.stemText: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the journalist's argument?

options.optionContent: It fails to provide a definition of the terms "honesty" and "integrity."
It fails to show that only those who produce art pervaded with honesty are honest artists.
Its claim that the filmmaker's art is uncommercial does not prove that the films did not yield significant taxable profits.
It fails to show that John Dowland was not also involved in commercial ventures.
It fails to justify its presumption that personal dishonesty and artistic honesty are incompatible.

items.stimulusText:
Olivia: Democracy in ancient Athens led to an unstable political climate, but Athenian democracy was very different from contemporary democracies. Contemporary democracies have corrected the flaws in the ancient Athenian system.

Karl: On the contrary, it should be obvious that contemporary democracies retain the defects of the ancient system. After all, many social problems—crime, unemployment, and poverty, to name a few—are common in democratic countries today.
items.stemText: The reasoning in Karl's argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

options.optionContent: overlooks the possibility that crime, unemployment, and poverty are common in countries that lack democratic forms of government
presumes, without providing justification, that crime, unemployment, and poverty were the only problems common in ancient Athens
overlooks the possibility that many contemporary societies are already taking steps to reduce their rates of crime and unemployment
fails to show that the problems with current political systems are worse than the problems with the ancient Athenian political system
overlooks the possibility that the social problems common in democratic countries today are not due to defects in those countries' political systems

items.stimulusText: Although annoying and a serious waste of paper, postal junk mail does not interfere with the recipient's mail service. By contrast, a large amount of junk e-mail can overload a recipient's e-mail service so that none of the recipient's e-mail is accessible. Thus, whether or not legal restrictions on postal junk mail are justified, there should be such restrictions on sending junk e-mail.
items.stemText: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the reasoning above?

options.optionContent: Legal restrictions should be placed on a form of communication only if one can negatively affect other people by using it.
Legal restrictions should be placed on any form of communication that might interfere with a recipient's ability to receive communication.
If legal restrictions on postal junk mail are justified, then legal restrictions should be placed on all forms of communication.
Legal restrictions can be placed on a form of communication even if it does not have the potential to produce serious problems for the whole society.
Legal restrictions should be placed on a form of communication only if doing so would make that form of communication more efficient.

items.stimulusText: Psychotherapist: Most social situations can be interpreted in a wide variety of ways, both positive and negative. Yet those who cannot formulate several possible interpretations of a situation are more likely to interpret the situation negatively and to feel sad about it. Hence, one of the most effective ways to avoid sadness is to learn to recognize and tolerate ambiguity.
items.stemText: Which one of the following is an assumption the psychotherapist's argument requires?

options.optionContent: Those who can formulate several possible interpretations of a situation are likely to see that situation as purely positive.
One of the most effective ways to avoid feeling sad about a situation is to understand that several interpretations of any situation are possible.
Those who have learned to recognize and tolerate ambiguity are more likely to be able to formulate several possible interpretations of a social situation.
To avoid feeling sad about a situation, it is necessary to recognize that there are several possible interpretations of that situation.
For a typical social situation, anyone who formulates only negative interpretations of that situation is likely to feel sad as a result.

items.stimulusText: Farmer: According to United Nations estimates, almost 250 million hectares of new cropland will be needed over the next 30 years to feed the world's growing population. Yet in the countries where population growth is most rapid, there are only about 50 million hectares of undeveloped land that could be turned to conventional agricultural use. There are, however, new agricultural techniques that make it possible to grow crops on a great deal of land unsuitable for conventional agriculture, so farmers clearly need to begin using these techniques if the increasing population is to be fed.
items.stemText: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the farmer's argument?

options.optionContent: The undeveloped land that could be used for conventional agriculture in the countries whose populations are growing most rapidly makes up the great majority of such land available worldwide.
In the countries whose populations are growing most rapidly, much of the land that is unsuitable for conventional agriculture is developed.
If farmers begin growing crops on all of the land unsuitable for conventional agriculture, enough food could be produced to feed the world's population over the next 30 years.

The new agricultural techniques that make it possible to grow crops on land that is unsuitable for conventional agriculture also make it possible for crops to be grown on almost any land that is suitable for conventional agriculture.
Over the next 30 years, the countries whose populations are growing most rapidly are likely to discover new techniques for feeding their growing populations.

items.stimulusText: People have informal obligations both to other people and to institutions. An obligation to the government is different from any of these. When you have an obligation to the government, that obligation is encoded in law. If you break a law, representatives of the government can try to force you to do what the law demands. The legal right to initiate the use of force makes the government different from all other institutions.
items.stemText: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the conclusion of the argument as a whole?

options.optionContent: An obligation to the government is different from informal obligations to people and institutions.
When you have an obligation to the government, that obligation is encoded in law.
The legal right to initiate the use of force makes the government different from all other institutions.
Legal obligations to the government are backed by the government's legal right to initiate the use of force.
Obligations to the government, unlike obligations to other people and institutions, are encoded in law.

items.stimulusText: The incidence of collisions with large pieces of debris left in orbit from previous space missions is greatest for spacecraft such as communications satellites that orbit highest above the Earth. The reason is that there are more large pieces of debris there than closer to the Earth. The risk of serious damage by debris, however, is greater for spacecraft in low orbits than for spacecraft in high orbits, because ...
items.stemText: Which one of the following, if true, provides the best explanation to complete the passage?

options.optionContent: high-speed collisions with small pieces of debris, which can cause irreparable ruptures in spacecraft surfaces, are more frequent for spacecraft in lower orbits than for spacecraft in higher orbits
some spacecraft launched into orbit complete their missions and return to Earth without leaving any debris in either high or low orbits
telescopes and radar can detect the path of debris with better accuracy for debris orbiting closer to the Earth than for debris in higher orbits
communications satellites cannot be maneuvered around large pieces of debris, but piloted spacecraft such as space shuttles, which orbit closer to the Earth, can avoid such hazards
spacecraft orbiting the Earth are more likely to collide with orbiting debris than with objects such as meteoroids, which pass by the Earth relatively quickly

items.stimulusText: Before the advent of modern statehood and mass media, languages with common roots blended into one another much more than they do today. There was, for example, a smoothly graded continuum of dialects from the "French" spoken in Paris to the "Italian" spoken in Rome. Today, clear political boundaries and mass media have resulted in distinct, homogeneous languages. In biology, we observe a similar phenomenon: Where populations are spread evenly over a large area, some species blend into one another. Thus, where populations have become geographically isolated from one another, we should expect to find ...
items.stemText: Which one of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.optionContent: clearly distinct species
smooth continuums between species
minor physical differences from organism to organism
a lack of homogeneity within populations
species that have no relationship to one another

items.stimulusText:
Principle: If a significant hardship is caused by obeying a subpoena—a court order to present evidence—the court should nullify the subpoena unless the party on whose behalf the subpoena was issued convinces the court that evidence relevant to its case could be acquired only through that subpoena.

Application: When a professional writer was ordered by subpoena to release an unpublished article to the court, the court nullified the subpoena because it was convinced that the article would be published before the trial date, thereby becoming available to everyone.
items.stemText: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to justify the above application of the principle?

options.optionContent: The party on whose behalf the subpoena was issued was a source of some of the information found in the unpublished article.
The writer has no information relevant to the case other than what is contained in the article.
The article would no longer be publishable if it were revealed to the court.
Several revelations in the article are clearly relevant to the case in question.
The writer and two magazine editors are the only people who have full knowledge of the article's contents.

description: The Drill Set contains logical reasoning question types. Each question involves analyzing a short passage and evaluating its reasoning, emphasizing critical thinking skills pivotal in legal reasoning.
